text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

Channel 4 will show the entire event in their biggest TV event ever

Paris is about to play host to the biggestParalympic Gamesever.

ParalympicsGB finished second behind China inTokyothree years ago, winning 41 golds and 124 medals, and will have hopes for similar success over the coming weeks.

WhileTeam GBcould only finish seventh in the recentOlympics, trailing Australia, France and Japan despite winning more medals, Britain have one of the leading global para-sport programmes.

Here’s how to keep up with all the action in the French capital over the next fortnight.

Channel 4 will air the entire Games for free across the TV channel, More4, Channel 4 streaming and the channel’s sportYouTube.

They will broadcast over 1300 hours of live sport across 12 days, including the opening and closing ceremonies, with up to 18 streams online at any one time and a highlights show.

If you’re looking to get in the mood before the action begins, Channel 4 are currently airing a host of Paralympics documentaries on the athletes and their stories.

The Paris 2024 Paralympics will take place from Wednesday 28 August to Sunday 8 September, with the action starting in earnest on Thursday after the opening ceremony on Wednesday evening – the first taking place outside a stadium.

Like the Olympics, the opening ceremony will take place across iconic Parisian venues, rather than just in one stadium and will be broadcast from 7.30pm to 10.30pm, presented by Ade Adepitan, Clare Balding and Ellie Simmonds.

As is the case every night of the Games, that will then be followed by an edition of The Last Leg with Adam Hills, Alex Brooker and Josh Widdicombe. This will take place at 10.30pm on Wednesday, then 9.30pm every other evening until Saturday 7 September.

The closing ceremony will be broadcast from 8pm-11pm on Sunday 8 September, immediately followed by the last leg.

Throughout the Games, coverage will start from 8am, with the breakfast show presented by Billy Monger and Vick Hope.

JJ Chalmers, one of the faces of the recent Olympics, will then take over for the Daytime Live show from 11am-3pm, before Balding and Rose Ayling-Ellis will helm the afternoon edition.

Balding will then return for the evening show alongside Adepitan and Simmonds, before The Last Leg and a highlights show with Josh Pugh and Gaz Choudhury from 10.30pm end the day’s action.

The Paralympics is a wonderful opportunity to watch some different sports, or fresh takes on classics.

There are 22 sports in Paris, with 549 gold medals up for grabs across differing classifications.

Goalball, boccia and sitting volleyball are all unique to the Paralympics, while events like blind football and wheelchair-based team sports are adaptations of more common events.

Day 0: Wednesday28 AugThere is no sport, just the Opening Ceremony. Thankfully there will be no boat parade down the River Seine but organisers are once again flouting tradition by staging it down the Champs-Élysées and at Place de la Concorde.

Thomas Jolly, artistic director for the ceremonies, promises performances that have never been seen before as Paris puts on the first Paralympic Opening Ceremony outside of a stadium.ParalympicsGB’s flagbearers are set to be announced today and will follow swimmer Ellie Simmonds and archer John Stubbs.

Day 1: Thursday29 AugThis is the first day that medals are up for grabs, with 66 to be won.

The velodrome takes centre stage from a British perspective with Kadeena Cox, a medallist in athletics and cycling, in action in the time trial alongside legendary tandem sprinter Neil Fachie in the pursuit.

In the pool, Tully Kearney has the chance to go one better in the heats and finals of the S5 women’s 200m freestyle – in which she won silver in Tokyo – which will also feature Suzanna Hext, who has previously competed internationally in para dressage.

Other sports begin their campaigns, including para archery, where Jodie Grinham competes in the women’s individual compound open ranking round while 28 weeks pregnant.

Day 2: Friday 30 AugAll eyes will be on the pool with Maisie Summers-Newton defending her title in the 200m individual medley.

Twins Eliza and Scarlett Humphrey will do battle in the S11 women’s 400m freestyle final also at La Défense Arena.At the velodrome, Jaco van Gass and Fin Graham won gold and silver and both broke the world record in Tokyo and will hope for more of the same on the fast Parisian track.

Tim Jeffery and Ryan Cockbill will compete in the Mixed 10m standing SH2 final, with shooting para sport the only sport ParalympicsGB did not win a medal in last time out.

Day 3: Saturday 31 AugUnder the shadow of Napoleon’s tomb at Les Invalides, Phoebe Paterson Pine will be one of the favourites in the compound archery.

Hopes are high in taekwondo, too, with Matt Bush finally getting his chance after narrowly missing out on Tokyo due to a last-minute injury.

Thomas Young will go in the men’s T38 100m final at the athletics as he looks to get the second of a target of four Paralympic golds in the event with Sophie Hahn and Olivia Breen going in the women’s event half an hour later.

ParalympicsGB’s youngest competitor, Iona Winnifrith makes her Games debut aged 13 as she goes in the women’s SM7 200m individual medley.

Day 4: Sunday 1 SepParalympicsGB have the best rowing team in the world and this is their day to shine, with the mixed coxed four protecting an unbeaten record that stretches back to London 2012.

Lauren Rowles can also become the first woman to win three Para rowing golds as she goes in the mixed doubles sculls with new partner Gregg Stevenson.

Former Strictly contestant Lauren Steadman competes in the triathlon, alongside Claire Cashmore.

Hannah Cockroft will go for a historic four-peat in the 100m which could see a British 1-2-3 with Kare Adenegan and Fabienne Andre also competing.

In boccia, Claire Taggart goes in search of her first medal in the sport in the women’s BC2, with Kayleigh Haggo also competing.

Day 5: Monday 2 SepGB stunned the world to be crowned “Murderball” champions in Tokyo, their first-ever medal in wheelchair rugby. Can they go back-to-back?

Rachel Choong and Jack Shephard could win SH6 mixed doubles gold in the badminton, with neither having won a Paralympic medal before.

One of the young stars of Tokyo 2020, Ellie Challis is still only 20 but has a chance to win a second medal in the women’s S3 50m backstroke having won silver three years ago.

Blade runner Jonnie Peacock takes undisputed centre stage in the evening and will be hellbent on regaining his 100m crown.

Day 6: Tuesday 3 SepEquestrian events begin in the stunning surroundings of Château de Versailles, the former seat of the French monarchy, with Georgia Wilson and Natasha Baker returning to the Paralympic stage.

Medals will be won in one of Paris’ most jaw-dropping venues, the Grand Palais, with Dimitri Coutya, Piers Gilliver and Gemma Collis-McCann all in action.

It is another night of finals in the pool and on the track, with the “Weirwolf’ David Weir still going strong aged 45 and entered in the 1500m wheelchair race.

Faye Rogers goes in the women’s S10 100m butterfly in the swimming, competing at the Paralympics having previously raced at the Olympic trials in the same sport.

Day 7: Wednesday 4 SepCyclist Dame Sarah Storey, Britain’s greatest Paralympian, will break another record in competing at her ninth Games starting with the time trial on the road.

There are 10 other cyclists in actions in the time trial, which starts and ends in the suburb of Clichy-Sous-Bois.

GB won their first wheelchair fencing gold medal in 32 years in Tokyo and there are medal shots for Oliver Lam-Watson and Dimitri Coutya in the foil.

Para powerlifting begins on Wednesday, with Zoe Newson the first British athlete to go in the women’s 45kg. She won bronze at Rio and Tokyo and is competing in her fourth Games.

Day 8: Thursday 5 SepBoccia, similar to boules, is the only Paralympic sport without an Olympic equivalent. GB have a great medal chance in the mixed event, which will feature “the Ronnie O’Sullivan of boccia”, David Smith.

Daniel Greaves will go for an unprecedented seventh medal in the men’s F64 discus throw, which will also feature former rugby player Harrison Walsh, with Daniel Pembroke defending his F13 javelin gold.

Brother and sister Matthew and Rebecca Redfern will each compete in the men’s and women’s SB13 100m breaststroke.

Day 9: Friday 6 SepTable tennis showman Will Bayley, who famously climbed on the table in celebrating gold at Rio 2016, will aim for the top step in the singles final.

Jonathan Broom-Edwards defends his high jump title and there is a second and final shot at gold for Storey in the road race, possibly her last-ever Paralympic event.

One of the most interesting events in the athletics programme also takes place in the form of the 4x100m universal relay which features a visually impaired athlete, an amputee sprinter, an athlete with cerebral palsy and finally a wheelchair racer.

Day 10: Saturday 7 SepThis has all the making of a super Saturday for ParalympicsGB.

Wheelchair tennis star Alfie Hewett owns every accolade in the sport other than a Paralympic gold medal with the men’s singles final set for the iconic Roland-Garros.

The Flying Mullet will be in action as Zak Skinner, and his long blonde locks, competes in the men’s T13 long jump final.

Chris Skelley goes for judo gold, Charlotte Henshaw and whole host of medal chances paddle for glory in canoeing and GB will hope to be involved in the men’s wheelchair basketball medal matches.

Stephen Clegg has never won a Paralympic gold and his best chance may come in GB’s final event in the pool, the men’s S12 100m butterfly, it could be his third medal of the Games and if so, it would see him eclipse sister Libby who won five medals in athletics.

Day 11: Sunday 8 SepThe final day of the Games sees the marathon wind around the streets of Paris, the discipline in which Weir specialises these days.

The women’s event will feature Eden Rainbow-Cooper, who became the first woman to win the Boston Marathon this year.There are further medal chances for GB in canoeing, with former Mr England Jack Eyers making his Paralympic debut and powerlifting.

GB’s women could win their first-ever Paralympic medal in wheelchair basketball before the curtain comes down at the closing ceremony at Stade de France.

title: Paralympics 2024: How to watch, TV timings and day-by-day schedule
author: George Simms, Sportsbeat
description: Channel 4 will show the entire event in their biggest TV event ever
